content:
A random variable $X$ has the following probability distribution :

$$ \begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{X}=x & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=x) & 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.4 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

The mean and standard deviation of $X$ are respectively
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the Mean

The mean of $X$ is found by multiplying each value of $X$ by its probability and then adding up all the results.

$$ \text{Mean} = \mathrm{E}(X) = \sum x_i \cdot \mathrm{P}(x_i) $$

So, $$ = 1 \times 0.1 + 2 \times 0.2 + 3 \times 0.3 + 4 \times 0.4 $$

$$ = 0.1 + 0.4 + 0.9 + 1.6 = 3 $$

Step 2: Calculate the Variance

First, find $\mathrm{E}\left(X^2\right)$ by multiplying each value squared by its probability and adding up the results:

$$ \mathrm{E}(X^2) = 1^2 \times 0.1 + 2^2 \times 0.2 + 3^2 \times 0.3 + 4^2 \times 0.4 $$

$$ = 1 \times 0.1 + 4 \times 0.2 + 9 \times 0.3 + 16 \times 0.4 $$

$$ = 0.1 + 0.8 + 2.7 + 6.4 = 10 $$

Now, use the formula:

$$ \operatorname{Var}(X) = \mathrm{E}(X^2) - [\mathrm{E}(X)]^2 $$

$$ = 10 - (3)^2 = 10 - 9 = 1 $$

Step 3: Find the Standard Deviation

Standard deviation is the square root of variance.

$$ \text{S.D.} = \sqrt{1} = 1 $$

content:
The probability distribution of a random variable X is given by

$$ \begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{X}=x_i & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{X}=x_i\right) & 0.4 & 0.3 & 0.1 & 0.1 & 0.1 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Then the variance of X is

Let mean and standard deviation of probability distribution

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{X}=x & -3 & 0 & 1 & \alpha \\ \hline \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=x) & \frac{1}{4} & \mathrm{~K} & \frac{1}{4} & \frac{1}{3} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

be $\mu$ and $\sigma$ respectively and if $\sigma-\mu=2$ then $\sigma=$
explanation:
The sum of all the probabilities in a probability distribution is always unity.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{4}+K+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{3}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow K+\frac{5}{6}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow K=\frac{1}{6} \\ & \text { Mean }(\mu)=-3\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+0+1\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+\alpha\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{\alpha}{3}-\frac{1}{2} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \begin{aligned} \sigma^2 & =(-3)^2\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+0+1^2\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+\alpha^2\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)-\mu^2 \\ & =\frac{10}{4}+\frac{\alpha^2}{3}-\left(\frac{\alpha}{3}-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{5}{2}+\frac{\alpha^2}{3}-\frac{\alpha^2}{9}+\frac{\alpha}{3}-\frac{1}{4} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \sigma^2=\frac{2 \alpha^2}{9}+\frac{\alpha}{3}+\frac{9}{4} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ & \sigma-\mu=2\,\,\,\,\, …[Given]\\ & \Rightarrow \sigma=2+\mu \\ & \Rightarrow \sigma=\frac{\alpha}{3}+\frac{3}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\, …[From (i)]\\ & \Rightarrow \sigma^2=\frac{\alpha^2}{9}+\alpha+\frac{9}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 \alpha^2}{9}+\frac{\alpha}{3}+\frac{9}{4}=\frac{\alpha^2}{9}+\alpha+\frac{9}{4} \,\,\,…[From (ii)]\\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha^2}{9}-\frac{2 \alpha}{3}=0 \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \alpha^2-6 \alpha=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha(\alpha-6)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=0 \text { or } \alpha=6 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=6 \\ & \mu=\frac{6}{3}-\frac{1}{2} \,\,\, ...[From (i)]\\ & =\frac{3}{2} \\ & \sigma-\mu=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \sigma-\frac{3}{2}=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \sigma=\frac{7}{2} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { } \end{aligned} $$

content: The mean and variance of seven observations are 8 and 16 respectively. If five of the observations are $2,4,10,12,14$, then the product of remaining two observations is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let the unknown numbers be } x \text { and } y \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Mean }=8 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2+4+10+12+14+x+y}{7}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=14 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { Variance }=16 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2^2+4^2+10^2+12^2+14^2+x^2+y^2}{7} -(\text { mean })^2=16 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 460+x^2+y^2=7\left[16+(8)^2\right] \\ & \Rightarrow 460+x^2+y^2=560 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=100\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$x=6, y=8 \text { or } x=8, y=6$$

$\therefore\quad$ Product $=48$

content:
The cumulative distribution function of a discrete random variable X is given by

$\mathrm{X}=x$ $-1$ $0$ $1$ $2$
$\mathrm{F(X=x)}$ 0.3 0.7 0.8 1

Then $\mathrm{E(X^2)=}$
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=-1)=\mathrm{F}(-1)=0.3 \\ & \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=0)=\mathrm{F}(0)-\mathrm{F}(-1)=0.7-0.3=0.4 \\ & \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=1)=\mathrm{F}(1)-\mathrm{F}(0)=0.8-0.7=0.1 \\ & \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=2)=\mathrm{F}(2)-\mathrm{F}(1)=1-0.8=0.2 \\ & \mathrm{E}\left(\mathrm{X}^2\right)=\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2 \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(x_{\mathrm{i}}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$\begin{aligned} & =(-1)^2(0.3)+0^2(0.4)+1^2(0.1)+2^2(0.2) \\ & =0.3+0+0.1+0.8 \\ & =1.2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the sum of the deviations of 50 observations from 30 is 50 , then the mean of these observations is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let the observations be }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots x_{50} \\ & \text { Then, } \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{50}\left(x_{\mathrm{i}}-30\right)=50 \\ & \Rightarrow \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{50} x_{\mathrm{i}}-(30 \times 50)=50 \\ & \Rightarrow \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{50} x_{\mathrm{i}}=1550 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Mean }=\frac{\sum_\limits{\mathrm{i}=1}^{50} x_i}{n}=\frac{1550}{50}=31 \end{aligned}$$

content: Variance of first n natural numbers is ________.
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \sigma^2 & =\frac{1}{n}\left(1^2+2^2+3^2+\ldots+n^2\right) -\left(\frac{1+2+3+\ldots+n}{n}\right)^2\\ & =\frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}\right)-\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2 n}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}-\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{n+1}{2}\left(\frac{2 n+1}{3}-\frac{n+1}{2}\right) \\ & =\frac{n+1}{2}\left(\frac{n-1}{6}\right) \\ & =\frac{n^2-1}{12} \end{aligned}$$

content: The mean of the numbers $a, b, 8,5,10$ is 6 and the variance is $6.8$ . Then which of the following gives possible values of $a$ and $b$ ?
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Mean }=6 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{a+b+8+5+10}{5}=6 \\ & \Rightarrow a+b=7 \\ & \Rightarrow(a-6)=(1-b)\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} & 6.80=\frac{\sum\left(x_i-\bar{x}\right)^2}{n} \\ & \Rightarrow 6.80=\frac{(a-6)^2+(b-6)^2+4+1+16}{5} \\ & \Rightarrow 34=(a-6)^2+(b-6)^2+21 \\ & \Rightarrow(a-6)^2+(b-6)^2=13 \\ & \Rightarrow(1-b)^2+(b-6)^2=13\quad\text{.... [From (i)]} \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow b^2-2 b+1+b^2-12 b+36=13 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 b^2-14 b+24=0 \\ & \Rightarrow b^2-7 b+12=0 \\ & \Rightarrow b=3,4 \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad b=3 \Rightarrow a=4 \text { and } \\ b=4 \Rightarrow a=3 \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

content:
If for some $x \in \mathbb{R}^{+} \cup\{0\}$, the frequency distribution of the marks obtained by 20 students in a test is

Marks : 2 3 5 7
Frequency : $(x+1)^2$ $2x-5$ $x^2-3x$ $x$

then the mean of the marks is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Here, } \Sigma \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{i}}=(x+1)^2+2 x-5+x^2-3 x+x \\ & \quad=2 x^2+2 x-4 \\ & \begin{aligned} \Sigma \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{i}} x_{\mathrm{i}} & =2(x+1)^2+3(2 x-5)+5\left(x^2-3 x\right)+7 x \\ & =7 x^2+2 x-13 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{N}=20\quad \text{...[Given]}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sum \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{i}}=20 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x^2+2 x-4=20 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-4,3 \\ & \Rightarrow x=3\quad \ldots\left[\because x \in \mathrm{R}^{+} \cup\{0\}\right] \end{aligned}$$

Now mean $(\bar{x})=\frac{\sum \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{i}} x_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{N}}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{7(3)^2+2(3)-13}{20} \\ & =2.8 \end{aligned}$$

content: In an experiment with 15 observations for $x$, the following results were available $\sum x^2=2830, \sum x=170$. One observation 20 was found to be wrong and was replaced by the correct value 30 . Then the corrected variance is
explanation:
Given: $\sum x=170$ and $\sum x^2=2830$

Corrected sum

$$\begin{aligned} & \sum x=170-20+30=180 \\ & \sum x^2=2830-400+900=3330 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Corrected variance

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{\sum x^2}{\mathrm{n}}\right)-\left(\frac{\sum x}{\mathrm{n}}\right)^2 \\ & =\left(\frac{3330}{15}\right)-\left(\frac{180}{15}\right)^2 \\ & =222-144=78 \end{aligned}$$

content: The mean and variance of 7 observations are 8 and 16 respectively. If first five observations are $2,4,10,12,14$, then absolute difference of remaining two observations is
explanation:
Let the unknown numbers be $x$ and $y$.

Mean = 8

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{2+4+10+12+14+x+y}{7}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=14 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { Variance }=16 \end{aligned}$$

$\Rightarrow \frac{2^2+4^2+10^2+12^2+14^2+x^2+y^2}{7} -(\text { mean })^2=16$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 460+x^2+y^2=7\left[16+(8)^2\right] \\ & \Rightarrow 460+x^2+y^2=560 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=100\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

Solving (i) and (ii), we get

$$x=6, y=8 \text { or } x=8, y=6$$

$\therefore\quad$ Required absolute difference $=|x-y|=2$

content: Mean and variance of six observations are 6 and 12 respectively. If each observation is multiplied by 3, then new variance of the resulting observations is
explanation:
When each item of a data is multiplied by $\lambda$,

Variance is multiplied by $\lambda^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\quad\text { New variance } & =3^2 \times 12 \\ & =9 \times 12 \\ & =108 \end{aligned}$$

content: The variance of 20 observations is 5 . If each observation is multiplied by 3 and then 8 is added to each number, then variance of resulting observations is
explanation:
When each item of a data is multiplied by $\lambda$, variance is multiplied by $\lambda^2$.

$\therefore \quad$ New variance $=3^2 \times 5=9 \times 5=45$

Since 8 is added to each number, the variance remains the same i.e. 45

content:
If X is a random variable with distribution given below

$\mathrm{X}=x_{\mathrm{i}}:$ 0 1 2 3
$\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{X}=x_{\mathrm{i}}\right):$ $\mathrm{k}$ $\mathrm{3k}$ $\mathrm{3k}$ $\mathrm{k}$

Then the value of $k$ and its variance are respectively given by
explanation:
The sum of all the probabilities in a probability distribution is always unity.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}+3 \mathrm{k}+3 \mathrm{k}+\mathrm{k}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow 8 \mathrm{k}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow k=\frac{1}{8} \\ & \mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X})=\sum x_1 \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(x_1\right) \\ & =0\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)+1\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)+2\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)+3\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)=\frac{3}{2} \\ & \operatorname{Var}(\mathrm{X})=\mathrm{E}\left(\mathrm{X}^2\right)-[E(\mathrm{X})]^2 \\ & =0^2\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)+1^2\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)+2^2\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)+3^2\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)-\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: The mean and the standard deviation of 10 observations are 20 and 2 respectively. Each of these 10 observations is multiplied by p and then reduced by $q$, where $p \neq 0$ and $q \neq 0$. If the new mean and new standard deviation (s.d.) become half of the original values, then $q$ is equal to
explanation:
Mean $=20$ and $S D=2, n=10$

If each observation is multiplied by $p$ and then reduced by q.

New Mean $=\bar{x}_1=\mathrm{p} \bar{x}-\mathrm{q}$

$$\begin{aligned} & 10=p(20)-q \\ & \Rightarrow 20 p-q=10 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { New } S D=\sigma_1=|p| \sigma \end{aligned}$$

Squaring on both sides,

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 1=\mathrm{p}^2 \times 4 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}^2=\frac{1}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}= \pm \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

From (i)

When, $\mathrm{p}=\frac{-1}{2}, \mathrm{q}=-20$

When, $\mathrm{p}=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{q}=0$

content: The mean and variance of seven observations are 8 and 16 respectively. If 5 of the observations are $2,4,10,12,14$, then the square root of product of remaining two observations is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Let the unknown numbers be } x \text { and } y \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Mean }=8 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2+4+10+12+14+x+y}{7}=8 \\ & \Rightarrow x+y=14 \quad\text{... (i)}\\ & \text { Variance }=16 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2^2+4^2+10^2+12^2+14^2+x^2+y^2}{7} -(\text { mean })^2=16 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 460+x^2+y^2=7\left[16+(8)^2\right] \\ & \Rightarrow 460+x^2+y^2=560 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=100\quad\text{... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Solving (i) and (ii), we get } \\ & x=6, y=8 \text { or } x=8, y=6 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Product }=48 \\ & \therefore \quad \sqrt{\text { Product }}=\sqrt{48} ~~4 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: The variance of first 50 even natural numbers is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Variance }=\frac{\sum x_i^2}{n}-(\bar{x})^2 \\ & =\frac{\left(2^2+4^2+\ldots+100^2\right)}{50}-\left(\frac{2+4+\ldots .+100}{50}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{4\left(1^2+2^2+\ldots .+50^2\right)}{50}-(51)^2 \\ & =4\left(\frac{50 \times 51 \times 101}{50 \times 6}\right)-(51)^2 \\ & =3434-2601 \\ & =833 \end{aligned}$$

content: A student scores the following marks in five tests : $54,45,41,43,57$. His score is not known for the sixth test. If the mean score is 48 in six tests, then the standard deviation of marks in six tests is
explanation:
Let students' sixth test score be $x$,

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { Mean }=48 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{54+45+41+43+57+x}{6}=48 \\ & \Rightarrow x=48 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ Standard deviation

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum\left(x_1-\bar{x}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{1}{6} \times\left[\left(6^2+(-3)^2+(-7)^2+(-5)^2+(9)^2\right)\right]} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{200}{6}} \\ & =\frac{10}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The mean of $n$ observations is $\bar{x}$. If three observations $\mathrm{n}+1, \mathrm{n}-1,2 \mathrm{n}-1$ are added such that mean remains same, then value of $n$ is
explanation:
Let the n observations be $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{\mathrm{n}}$

$\therefore \quad$ According to have given condition, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{x_1+x_2+\ldots+x_n}{n} \\ & =\frac{x_1+x_2+\ldots+x_n+n+1+n-1+2 n-1}{n+3} \\ \therefore & \bar{x}=\frac{x_1+x_2+\ldots+x_n+4 n-1}{n+3} \\ \therefore & \bar{x}=\frac{\left(\frac{x_1+x_2+\ldots+x_n}{n}\right)+4 \mathrm{n}-1}{n+3} \\ \therefore & \bar{x}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \bar{x}+4 \mathrm{n}-1}{\mathrm{n}+3} \\ \therefore & \bar{n} \bar{x}+3 \bar{x}=\mathrm{n} \bar{x}+4 \mathrm{n}-1 \\ \therefore & 3 \bar{x}=4 \mathrm{n}-1 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{n}=\frac{3 \bar{x}+1}{4} \end{array}$$

content: Consider three observations $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and c such that $b=a+c$. If the standard deviation of $\mathrm{a}+2, \mathrm{~b}+2, \mathrm{c}+2$ is d , then ............ holds.
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Mean of } a, b, c \text { is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \bar{x}=\frac{a+b+c}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \bar{x}=\frac{2 b}{3} \end{aligned}\\ &\ldots[\because b=a+c] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { S.D. of } a+2, b+2, c+2= \text{S. D. of a, b, c}\\ \therefore\quad & d=\sqrt{\frac{a^2+b^2+c^2}{3}-\left(\frac{2 b}{3}\right)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow d^2=\frac{a^2+b^2+c^2}{3}-\frac{4 b^2}{9} \\ & \Rightarrow d^2=\frac{3\left(a^2+b^2+c^2\right)-4 b^2}{9} \\ & \Rightarrow 9 d^2=3\left(a^2+c^2\right)-b^2 \\ & \Rightarrow b^2=3\left(a^2+c^2\right)-9 d^2 \end{aligned}$$

content:
A random variable $X$ has the following probability distribution

$\mathrm{X=}x_i$: 1 2 3 4
$\mathrm{P(X=}x_i)$ : 0.2 0.4 0.3 0.1

The mean and variance of X are respectively
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Mean } & =\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X})=\sum x_{\mathrm{i}} \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(x_{\mathrm{i}}\right) \\ & =1(0.2)+2(0.4)+3(0.3)+4(0.1) \\ & =2.3 \\ \operatorname{Var}(\mathrm{X}) & =\mathrm{E}\left(\mathrm{X}^2\right)-[\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X})]^2 \\ & =1^2(0.2)+2^2(0.4)+3^2(0.3)+4^2(0.1) \\ & =0.2+1.6+2.7+1.6-5.29 \\ & =0.81 \end{aligned}$$

content: The mean of 100 observations is 50 and their standard deviation is 5 , then the sum of all squares of all the observations is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \bar{x}=50, \text { Standard deviation }=5 \\ & \mathrm{n}=100 \\ & \text { Mean } \bar{x}=50 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sum x_i}{100}=50 \\ & \Rightarrow \sum x_{\mathrm{i}}=5000 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \text { Now, standard deviation }=\sqrt{\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2}{\mathrm{n}}-\left(\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{n}}\right)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow 5=\sqrt{\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2}{100}-\left(\frac{5000}{100}\right)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow 25=\frac{\sum x_i^2}{100}-(50)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2}{100}=25+2500 \\ & \Rightarrow \sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2=252500 \end{aligned}$$

content: If both mean and variance of 50 observations $$x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{50}$$ are equal to 16 and 256 respectively, then mean of $$\left(x_1-5\right)^2,\left(x_2-5\right)^2, \ldots \ldots,\left(x_{50}-5\right)^2$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Mean }=\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & \Rightarrow 16=\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}}{50} \\ & \Rightarrow \sum x_{\mathrm{i}}=800 \\ & \text { Variance }=\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2}{\mathrm{n}}-(\bar{x})^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 256=\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2}{50}-(16)^2 \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2}{50}= & 512 \\ \Rightarrow \sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2= & 25600 \\ \text { New mean } & =\frac{\sum\left(x_{\mathrm{i}}-5\right)^2}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & =\frac{\sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2-10 \sum x_{\mathrm{i}}+\sum 25}{50} \\ & =\frac{25600-10(800)+25(50)}{50} \\ & =\frac{18850}{50} \\ & =377 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The variance of 20 observations is 5. If each observation is multiplied by 2, then variance of resulting observations is
explanation:
Let's denote the original set of observations as \( X = \{x_1, x_2, ..., x_{20}\} \). The variance of these observations (\(Var(X)\)) is given as 5. Variance is a measure of how spread out the values in a dataset are, and it is calculated as the average of the squared differences from the mean:

$$ Var(X) = \frac{\sum\limits_{i=1}^{20} (x_i - \mu)^2}{20} $$

where $ \mu $ represents the mean of the original observations.

Now, if each observation is multiplied by 2, we get a new set of observations $ Y = \{2x_1, 2x_2, ..., 2x_{20}\} $. The new variance ($Var(Y)$) can be calculated as follows:

$$ Var(Y) = \frac{1}{20} \sum\limits_{i=1}^{20} (2x_i - \mu')^2 $$

where $ \mu' $ is the mean of the new observations. Since the mean of the new observations would also be twice the mean of the original observations (because each term in the sum is doubled), we have $ \mu' = 2\mu $.

Now let's substitute and note that $ (2x_i - 2\mu)^2 = 4(x_i - \mu)^2 $, which gives us:

$$ Var(Y) = \frac{1}{20} \sum\limits_{i=1}^{20} 4(x_i - \mu)^2 $$

$$ Var(Y) = 4 \times \frac{1}{20} \sum\limits_{i=1}^{20} (x_i - \mu)^2 $$

$$ Var(Y) = 4 \times Var(X) $$

Since we know $ Var(X) = 5 $, we can now calculate $ Var(Y) $ :

$$ Var(Y) = 4 \times 5 = 20 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is

Option D : 20

content: Variance of first $$2 n$$ natural numbers is
explanation:
We know that, mean of the first $$2 n$$ natural numbers $$=\frac{1+2+3+\ldots+2 n}{2 n}=\frac{2 n+1}{2}$$

The variance is defined as the average of the squared differences from the mean.

Variance $$ = \left[ {{{{{(1 - mean)}^2} + {{(2 - mean)}^2} + \,...\, + \,{{(2n - mean)}^2}} \over {2n}}} \right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\frac{n(2 n+1)(4 n+1)-6 n(2 n+1)+6 n^2}{12 n}\right] \\ & =\frac{4 n^2-1}{12} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the sum of the mean and the variance of a binomial distribution for 5 trials is 1.8 , then the value of $$p$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Mean }=n p \\ & \text { Variance }=n p(1-p) \\ & \text { So, } \quad n p+n p(1-p)=1.8 \\ & 5 p+5 p(1-p)=1.8 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 10 p-5 p^2-1.8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 p^2-10 p+1.8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 p^2-9 p-p+1.8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 5 p(p-1.8)-1(p-1.8)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(p-1.8)(5 p-1)=0 \\ & \therefore \quad p=1.8,0.2 \\ & \because \quad p=1.8 \\ \end{aligned}$$

(It is not possible because it is not greater than 1)

Hence, p = 0.2

content:
The c.d.f. $$F(x)$$ associated with p.d.f. $$f(x)$$

$$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}12 x^2(1-x), & \text { if } 0< x <1 \\ 0 ; & \text { otherwise }\end{array}\right.$$ is
explanation:
$$\text { We have, } f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} 12 x^2(1-x), & \text { if } 0< x <1 \\ 0, & \text { otherwise } \end{array}\right.$$

$$F(x)=\int_\limits{-\infty}^x f(x) d x=\int_\limits{-\infty}^0 0 \cdot d x+\int_\limits0^x f(x) d x=\int_\limits0^x\left(12 x^2(1-x)\right) d x$$

$$F(x)=4 x^3-3 x^4$$

content: The raw data $$x_1, x_2, \ldots \ldots, x_{\mathrm{n}}$$ is an A.P. with common difference $$\mathrm{d}$$ and first term $$0, \bar{x}$$ and $$\sigma^2$$ are mean and variance of $$x_{\mathrm{i}}, \mathrm{i}=1,2, \ldots \ldots \mathrm{n}$$, then $$\sigma^2$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \bar{x} & =\frac{x_1+x_2+\ldots+x_{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & =\frac{\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}\left[2 x_1+(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}\right]}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & =\frac{(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}}{2} \quad ... [\because x_1=0]\\ \sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2 & =x_1^2+x_2^2+\ldots+x_{\mathrm{n}}^2 \\ & =0+\mathrm{d}^2+(2 \mathrm{~d})^2+\ldots+[(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}]^2\\ & =\mathrm{d}^2\left[1+2^2+3^2+\ldots+(\mathrm{n}-1)^2\right] \\ & =\mathrm{d}^2\left[\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)(2 \mathrm{n}-1)}{6}\right] \\ \therefore \quad \sigma^2 & =\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}} \sum x_{\mathrm{i}}^2-(\bar{x})^2 \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{d}^2(\mathrm{n}-1)(2 \mathrm{n}-1)}{6}-\left[\frac{(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}}{2}\right]^2 \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{d}^2(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{n}-1}{3}-\frac{\mathrm{n}-1}{2}\right) \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{d}^2(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}+1}{6}\right)=\frac{\left(\mathrm{n}^2-1\right) \mathrm{d}^2}{12} \end{aligned}$$

content: The discrete random variable $$\mathrm{X}$$ can take all possible integer values from 1 to $$\mathrm{k}$$, each with a probability $$\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}}$$, then its variance is
explanation:
$${x_i}$$ 1 2 3 --- $$k$$
$${p_i}$$ $${1 \over k}$$ $${1 \over k}$$ $${1 \over k}$$ --- $${1 \over k}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & E(X)=\sum_{i=1}^k x_i p_i=\frac{1}{k}+\frac{2}{k}+\ldots+\frac{k}{k}=\frac{k+1}{2} \\ & E\left(X^2\right)=\sum_{i=1}^k x_i^2 p_i=\frac{1^2+2^2+3^2+\ldots+k^2}{k} \\ & =\frac{(\mathrm{k}+1)(2 \mathrm{k}+1)}{6} \\ & \text { Variance }=E\left(X^2\right)-[E(X)]^2 \\ & =\frac{2 k^2+3 k+1}{6}-\frac{k^2+2 k+1}{4} \\ & \begin{aligned} & =\frac{4 k^2+6 k+2-3 k^2-6 k-3}{12} \\ & =\frac{k^2-1}{12} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: For 20 observations of variable $x$, if $$\sum\left(x_i-2\right)=20$$ and $$\sum\left(x_i-2\right)^2=100$$, then the standard deviation of variable $$x$$ is
explanation:
Note that standard derivation is independent of change of origin.

$$\therefore\quad$$ S.D. of $$x_i=$$ S.D. of $$\left(x_i-2\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { S.D. of } x_i=\text { S.D. of }\left(x_i-2\right) \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { S.D. of }\left(x_i-2\right) & =\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{20}\left(x_i-2\right)^2-\left[\frac{\sum\left(x_i-2\right)}{n}\right]^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{100}{20}-(1)^2} \\ & =2 \end{aligned} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Required S.D }=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the variance of the numbers $$-1,0,1, \mathrm{k}$$ is 5, where $$\mathrm{k} > 0$$, then $$\mathrm{k}$$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Variance }=\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2-\bar{x}^2 \\ & \text { Here, } \mathrm{n}=4 \text { and variance }=5 \\ & \therefore \quad 5=\frac{1}{4}\left[(-1)^2+(0)^2+(1)^2+k^2\right]-\left(\frac{-1+0+1+k}{4}\right)^2 \\ & \therefore \quad 5=\frac{2+\mathrm{k}^2}{4}-\frac{\mathrm{k}^2}{16} \\ & \therefore \quad 80=8+4 \mathrm{k}^2-\mathrm{k}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad 3 \mathrm{k}^2=72 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{k}^2=24 \\ & \therefore \quad k=2 \sqrt{6} \quad \ldots[\because \mathrm{k}>0] \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: If both mean and variance of 50 observations $$x_1, x_2, \ldots \ldots, x_{50}$$ are equal to 16 and 256 respectively, then mean of $$\left(x_1-5\right)^2,\left(x_2-5\right)^2, \ldots \ldots\left(x_{50}-5\right)^2$$ is
explanation:
Given that $$\mathrm{n}=50, \bar{x}=16$$ and $$\sigma_x^2=256$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \sigma_x^2=\frac{1}{n}\left(\sum_\limits{i=1}^{50} x_i^2\right)-(\bar{x})^2 \\ \therefore & 256=\frac{1}{50}\left(\sum_\limits{i=1}^{50} x_i^2\right)-256 \\ \therefore & \frac{1}{50}\left(\sum_\limits{i=1}^{50} x_i^2\right)=512 \\ \therefore & \sum_\limits{i=1}^{50} x_i^2=25600 \quad \text{..... (i)} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now } \sum_\limits{i=1}^{50}\left(x_i-5\right)^2=\sum_\limits{i=1}^{50} x_i^2+25 \times 50-10 \sum_\limits{i=1}^{50} x_i \\ & =25600+1250-8000 \quad \text {...[From (i) and (ii)] } \\ & =18850 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Required Mean }=\frac{\sum_\limits{\mathrm{i}=1}^{50}\left(x_{\mathrm{i}}-5\right)^2}{50}=\frac{18850}{50}=377 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If variance of $$x_1, x_2 \ldots \ldots, x_n$$ is $$\sigma_x^2$$, then the variance of $$\lambda x_1, \lambda x_2, \ldots \ldots, \lambda x_{\mathrm{n}}(\lambda \neq 0)$$ is
explanation:
When each item of a data is multiplied by $$\lambda$$, variance is multiplied by $$\lambda^2$$.

New variance $$=\lambda^2 \cdot \sigma_x^2$$

content: Mean and variance of six observations are 8 and 16 respectively. If each observation is multiplied by 3, then new variance of the resulting observations is
explanation:
When each item of a data is multiplied by $$\lambda$$, variance is multiplied by $$\lambda^2$$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { New variance } & =3^2 \times 16 \\ & =9 \times 16 \\ & =144 \end{aligned}$$

content: The variance, for first six prime numbers greater than 5, is
explanation:
First six prime numbers greater than 5 are 7,11, 13, 17, 19, 23.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Variance }\left(\sigma^2\right)=\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}}\left(\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} x_{\mathrm{i}}^2\right)-(\bar{x})^2 \\ & \therefore \quad \sigma^2=\frac{1}{6}\left(7^2+11^2+13^2+17^2+19^2+23^2\right) \\ & -\left(\frac{7+11+13+17+19+23}{6}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1518}{6}-\left(\frac{90}{6}\right)^2 \\ & =253-225 \\ & =28 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If the mean and S.D. of the data $$3,5,7, a, b$$ are 5 and 2 respectively, then $$a$$ and $$b$$ are the roots of the equation
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Mean }=5 \quad \text{.... [Given]}\\ & \therefore \quad \text { Mean }=\frac{\sum_\limits{i=1}^n x_i}{n} \\ & \Rightarrow 5=\frac{3+5+7+a+b}{5} \\ & \Rightarrow a+b=10 \quad \text{... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { S.D. }=2 \quad \text{... [Given]}\\ & \quad \text { S.D. }=\sqrt{\frac{\sum x_i^2}{n}-(x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow(2)^2=\frac{3^2+5^2+7^2+a^2+b^2}{5}-(5)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 4=\frac{83+a^2+b^2}{5}-25 \\ & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2=62 \quad \text{.... (ii)}\\ & \text { Now, (i) } \Rightarrow a+b=10 \\ & \text { Squaring both sides, we get } \\ & (a+b)^2=100 \\ & a^2+2 a b+b^2=100 \\ & 38=2 a b \quad \text{.... [From (ii)]}\\ \therefore & \quad \text { ab }=19 \\ & \text { Note that the required quadratic equation is expressed as } \\ & x^2-(a+b) x+a b=0 \\ \therefore & \quad x^2-10 x+19=0 \end{aligned}$$

content: A random variable $$\mathrm{X}$$ assumes values 1, 2, 3, ....., n with equal probabilities, if $$\operatorname{var}(X)=E(X)$$, then $$\mathrm{n}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{X}=1,2,3, \ldots . \mathrm{n} \\ & \begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}) & =\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}} \\ \mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X}) & =\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} x_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{i}} \\ & =\frac{(1+2+3+\ldots+\mathrm{n})}{\mathrm{n}} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2 \mathrm{n}} \\ \mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X}) & =\frac{\mathrm{n}+1}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{rl} \operatorname{Var}(X) & =\sum_\limits{i=1}^n x_i^2 p_i-[E(X)]^2 \\ & =\frac{1^2+2^2+3^2+\ldots+n^2}{n}-\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6 n}-\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}-\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2 \\ & \operatorname{Var}(X)=E(X) \quad \text{.... [Given]}\\ & \frac{(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}-\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{n+1}{2} \\ & \frac{2 n^2+n+2 n+1}{6}-\left(\frac{n^2+2 n+1}{4}\right)=\frac{n+1}{2} \\ & \frac{4 n^2+6 n+2-3 n^2-6 n-3}{12}=\frac{n+1}{2} \\ & n^2-1=6(n+1) \\ & n^2-1 =6 n+6 \\ & n^2-6 n-7 =0 \\ \therefore & n=-1 \text { or } n=7 \\ &\text { But } n \neq-1\\ \therefore & n=7 \end{array}$$

content:
The standard deviation of the following distribution

$$\mathrm{C.I.}$$ 0 - 6 6 - 12 12 - 18
$$\mathrm{f_i}$$ 2 4 6

is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{C.I.}$$ $$\mathrm{f_i}$$ $$\mathrm{x_i}$$ $$\mathrm{x_i^2}$$ $$\mathrm{f_i x_i}$$ $$\mathrm{f_i x_i^2}$$
0 - 6 2 3 9 6 18
6 - 12 4 9 81 36 324
12 - 18 6 15 225 90 1350
Total 12 132 1692

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Here } & \sum \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{i}}=12, \sum \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{i}} x_{\mathrm{i}}=132, \sum \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{i}} x_{\mathrm{i}}^2=1692 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{X}) & =\frac{1692}{12}-\left(\frac{132}{12}\right)^2 \\ & =141-121 \\ & =20 \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Standard deviation $$=\sqrt{20}=2 \sqrt{5}$$

content: The variance and mean of 15 observations are respectively 6 and 10 . If each observation is increased by 8 then the new variance and new mean of resulting observations are respectively
explanation:
When each observation of a data set is increased by a constant, the mean of the data set increases by that constant, but the variance does not change. Variance is a measure of the dispersion of the data points around the mean and is not affected by the addition (or subtraction) of a constant to each data point.

Let the original observations be denoted as $ x_1, x_2, ..., x_{15} $. The original mean ($ \mu $) is given by:

$$ \mu = 10 $$

The original variance ($ \sigma^2 $) is given by:

$$ \sigma^2 = 6 $$

Now, if each observation is increased by 8, the new observations will be:

$$ x'_i = x_i + 8 $$

for $ i = 1, 2, ..., 15 $. The new mean ($ \mu' $) of these observations will be:

$$ \mu' = \mu + 8 $$

$$ \mu' = 10 + 8 $$

$$ \mu' = 18 $$

The variance, however, is not affected by the addition of a constant to each data point. Therefore, the new variance ($ \sigma'^2 $) is the same as the original variance:

$$ \sigma'^2 = \sigma^2 $$

$$ \sigma'^2 = 6 $$

Hence, the new mean is 18 and the new variance is 6. The correct option is:

Option A

6, 18

content: For the set of 50 observations, the sum of their squares is 3050 , their arithmetic mean is 6. Hence the standard deviation of these observations is
explanation:
We have $$\mathrm{n}=50, \Sigma \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^2=3050, \overline{\mathrm{x}}=6$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { S.D. } & =\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum x_i^2-(\bar{x})^2} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{3050}{50}-(6)^2}=\sqrt{61-36}=\sqrt{25}=5 \end{aligned}$$

content: The arithmetic mean of marks in Mathematics for four divisions A, B, C and D were $$80,75,70$$ and 72 respectively. Their standard deviations were $$12,6,8$$ and 10 respectively. Then, division ______ has more uniformity.
explanation:
$$\text { We know that C.V. }=\frac{\text { Standard Deviation }}{\text { Mean }}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\mathrm{(C.V.)_A=\frac{12}{80}=0.15}$$ and $$\mathrm{(C.V.)_B=\frac{6}{75}=0.08}$$

$$\mathrm{(C.V.)_C=\frac{8}{70}=0.11}$$ and $$\mathrm{(C.V.)_D=\frac{10}{72}=0.14}$$

C.V. is the least for division B.

content: If the standard deviation of data is 12 and mean is 72, then coefficient of variation is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Coefficient of Variation } & =\frac{\text { Standard Deviation }}{\text { Mean }} \times 100 \% \\ & =\frac{12}{72} \times 100 \%=16.67 \% \end{aligned}$$

content: 140. Given that total of 16 values is 528 and sum of the squares of deviation from 33 is 9158. The variance is
explanation:
We have $$\mathrm{n}=16, \Sigma \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=528$$, and $$\frac{528}{16}=33 \Rightarrow \overline{\mathrm{x}}=33$$ and $$\Sigma\left(\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2=9158$$

Variance $$=\frac{1}{n} \Sigma\left(x_i-\bar{x}\right)^2=\frac{9158}{16}=572.375$$

content:
Following data shows the information about marks obtained in Physics, Chemistry, Mathematics and Biology by 100 students in a class. Then subject shows the highest variability in marks

Physics Chemistry Mathematics Biology
Mean 20 25 23 27
S.D. 3 2 4 5
explanation:
We know that Coefficient of Variation $$ = \mathrm{{{S\tan dard\,Deviation} \over {Mean}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${(C.V.)_{Physics}} = {3 \over {20}} = 0.15$$ and $${(C.V.)_{Chemistry}} = {2 \over {25}} = 0.08$$

$${(C.V.)_{Mathematics}} = {4 \over {23}} = 0.174$$ and $${(C.V.)_{Bio\log y}} = {5 \over {27}} = 0.185$$

content: If the variance of the data 2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 17 is 23.33, then the variance of 4, 8, 10, 12, 16, 34 will be
explanation:
Let's first understand the relationship between the given data sets. The second data set (4, 8, 10, 12, 16, 34) is a uniform scaling of the first data set (2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 17) by a factor of 2. This means each element in the second data set is obtained by multiplying the corresponding element in the first data set by 2.

The variance of a data set scaled by a constant $ c $ can be found by multiplying the original variance by $ c^2 $. In mathematical terms, if the variance of a data set $ X $ is $ \sigma^2 $, then the variance of the data set $ cX $ is given by:

$$\text{Variance}(cX) = c^2 \cdot \text{Variance}(X)$$

Given that the variance of the original data set $ X = (2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 17) $ is 23.33, we can calculate the variance of the scaled data set $ 2X $ as follows:

$$\text{Variance}(2X) = 2^2 \cdot 23.33 = 4 \cdot 23.33 = 93.32$$

Thus, the variance of the data set $ 4, 8, 10, 12, 16, 34 $ will be 93.32.

The correct option is Option A: 93.32.

content: The mean of five observations is 4 and their variance is 5.2. If three of these observations are 1, 2 and 6, then the other two are
explanation:
We have $$n=5, \bar{x}=4$$ and $$\sigma^2=5.2$$

Let the 5 observations be $$1,2,6$$, a, b

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore 4=\frac{1+2+6+a+b}{5} \Rightarrow a+b=11 \quad \text{.....(1)}\\ & \text { Variance }=\frac{\Sigma\left(x_i-\bar{x}\right)^2}{n} \\ & \therefore 5.2=\frac{(1-4)^2+(2-4)^2+(6-4)^2+(a-4)^2+(b-4)^2}{5} \\ & \therefore 26=9+4+4+(a-4)^2+(b-4)^2 \Rightarrow(a-4)^2+(b-4)^2=9 \quad \text{.....(2)} \end{aligned}$$

From (1), we get $$b=11-a$$ and substituting value of $$b$$ in (2), we write $$(a-4)^2+(7-a)^2=9 \Rightarrow a=4,7 \Rightarrow(a, b)$$ are 4 and 7

content: For X ~ B(n, p), if p = 0.6, E(X) = 6, then Var(X) =
explanation:
We have p = 0.6 and np = 6 $$\Rightarrow$$ n = 10

$$\therefore$$ Var (X) = npq = (10) (0.6) (0.4) = 2.4

content: A bakerman sells 5 types of cakes. Profit due to sale of each type of cake is respectively ₹ 2.5, ₹ 3 , ₹ 1.5 and ₹ 1. The demands for these cakes are $$20 \%, 5 \%, 10 \%, 50 \%$$ and respectively, then the expected profit per cake is
explanation:
Expected value $$=\Sigma \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =(2)\left(\frac{20}{100}\right)+(2.5)\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)+(3)\left(\frac{10}{100}\right)+(1.5)\left(\frac{50}{100}\right)+(1)\left(\frac{15}{100}\right) \\ & =0.4+0.125+0.3+0.75+0.15=1.725 \end{aligned}$$

content: For two data sets each of size 5 , the variance are given to be 4 and 5 and the corresponding means are given to be 2 and 4 respectively. The variance of the combined data set is
explanation:
We have $$\mathrm{n}_1=5, \sigma_1^2=4, \overline{\mathrm{x}}_1=2$$ and $$\mathrm{n}_2=5, \sigma_2^2=5, \overline{\mathrm{x}}_2=4$$

Here combined mean $$=\bar{x}_c=\frac{(5)(2)+(5)(4)}{(5)+(5)}=3$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{d}_1=\overline{\mathrm{x}}_1-\overline{\mathrm{x}}_{\mathrm{c}}=2-3=1 \text { and } \mathrm{d}_2=\overline{\mathrm{x}}_2-\overline{\mathrm{x}}_{\mathrm{c}}=4-3=1 \\ & \text { Combined variance }=\frac{\mathrm{n}_1\left(\sigma_1^2+\mathrm{d}_1^2\right)+\mathrm{n}_2\left(\sigma_2^2+\mathrm{d}_2^2\right)}{\mathrm{n}_1+\mathrm{n}_2}=\frac{5(4+1)+5(5+1)}{5+5}=\frac{11}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: If the variance of the numbers $$2,3,11$$ and $$x$$ is $$\frac{49}{4}$$, then the values of $$x$$ are
explanation:
Mean of given numbers is $$\frac{2+3+11+x}{4}$$ i.e. $$\frac{16+x}{4}$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Variance }=\frac{1}{n} \Sigma\left(\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2 \\ & \therefore \frac{49}{4}=\frac{1}{4}\left[\left(\frac{16+\mathrm{x}}{4}-2\right)^2+\left(\frac{16+x}{4}-3\right)^2+\left(\frac{16+x}{4}-11\right)^2+\left(\frac{16+x}{4}-x\right)^2\right] \\ & \therefore 49=\frac{(8+x)^2}{16}+\frac{(4+x)^2}{16}+\frac{(x-28)^2}{16}+\frac{(16-3 x)^2}{16} \\ & \therefore(49)(16)=\left(64+x^2+16 x\right)+\left(16+x^2+8 x\right)+\left(x^2-56 x+784\right)+\left(256+9 x^2-96 x\right) \\ & \therefore 784=12 x^2-128 x+80+784+256 \\ & \therefore 12 x^2-128 x+336=0 \quad \Rightarrow3 x^2-32 x+84=0 \\ & \therefore x=\frac{32 \pm \sqrt{(32)^2-(4)(3)(84)}}{\therefore}=\frac{32 \pm \sqrt{1024-1008}}{6}=\frac{32 \pm 4}{6} \\ & \therefore x=\frac{36}{6} \text { or } x=\frac{28}{6} \quad \Rightarrow x=6, \frac{14}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If 1 is added to first 10 natural numbers, then variance of the numbers so obtained is
If the standard deviation of the random variable $X$ is $\sqrt{3 p q}$ and mean is $3 p$ then $E\left(x^2\right)=\ldots \ldots$
explanation:
We have standard deviation of $X=\sqrt{3 p q}$

$$\Rightarrow \operatorname{Var}(X)=3 p q$$

and mean, $E(X)=3 p$

Now, $3 p q=E\left(x^2\right)-\left(3 p^2\right)$

$$\left(\because \operatorname{Var}(X)=E\left(X^2\right)-\left(E(X)^2\right)\right.$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad E\left(X^2\right) & =3 p q+9 p^2 \\ & =3 p(1-p)+9 p^2 \quad (\because p+q=1)\\ & =3 p-3 p^2+9 p^2 \\ \Rightarrow 3 p+6 p^2 & \Rightarrow 3 p(1+2 p) \end{aligned}\\ \end{aligned}$$
